---
title: "Telco Customer Churn — Predicting Churn and Monthly Charges"
subtitle: "End-to-end ML analysis: EDA, feature engineering, model ladder, and honest evaluation"
author: "Matej"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

## 1. Introduction

This report works through a full, leakage-safe machine learning pipeline on the
[Telco Customer Churn](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/blastchar/telco-customer-churn) dataset
(7,043 customers, 21 columns from a US telecom provider), answering two real-world questions:

- **Classification** — will a given customer **churn** (cancel their service)? Target: `Churn`.
- **Regression** — how much is a customer likely to be billed per month, i.e. what is their
  **`MonthlyCharges`**? Framed as: given a customer's plan and service mix, what monthly charge
  would we expect — useful for pricing sanity checks and flagging accounts that are billed
  unusually high or low for their service mix.

A single fixed random seed is used throughout for reproducibility.

```{python}
#| label: setup
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

RANDOM_STATE = 42
np.random.seed(RANDOM_STATE)

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 50)
```

## 2. Load & inspect the data

```{python}
#| label: load-data
df_raw = pd.read_csv("../data/WA_Fn-UseC_-Telco-Customer-Churn.csv")
print(df_raw.shape)
df_raw.head(3)
```

```{python}
#| label: dtypes
df_raw.dtypes
```

**Quirks found on inspection:**

- `customerID` is a unique identifier with no predictive value — dropped before modeling.
- `TotalCharges` is loaded as `object` (string), not numeric. This is because 11 customers with
  `tenure == 0` (brand-new customers) have a blank string `" "` instead of a number — pandas
  falls back to object dtype for the whole column.
- `SeniorCitizen` is encoded as `0`/`1` (already numeric) while most other binary features
  (`Partner`, `Dependents`, `PhoneService`, ...) are encoded as `"Yes"`/`"No"` strings.
- Several categorical columns use a 3-way encoding (e.g. `"No"`, `"Yes"`, `"No internet service"`)
  which is really "No" in disguise for customers without internet service at all.

```{python}
#| label: fix-total-charges
df_raw["TotalCharges"] = pd.to_numeric(df_raw["TotalCharges"], errors="coerce")
print(f"Rows with missing TotalCharges: {df_raw['TotalCharges'].isna().sum()}")
df_raw.loc[df_raw["TotalCharges"].isna(), ["tenure", "MonthlyCharges", "TotalCharges"]]
```

As suspected, every missing `TotalCharges` value belongs to a customer with `tenure == 0` —
they haven't been billed yet, so there is nothing to fill in from a "true" value. We treat this
as **missing-not-at-random but explainable**: it isn't noise, it's structural (new customer).

```{python}
#| label: missingness
df_raw.isna().sum().loc[lambda s: s > 0]
```

## 3. Cleaning

**Strategy for the 11 missing `TotalCharges` rows:** since `TotalCharges ≈ tenure × MonthlyCharges`
for this dataset, and `tenure == 0` for all of them, the mathematically consistent value is `0`.
We impute with `0` rather than a mean/median, because the mean would fabricate spending that
never happened for a customer who just signed up.

```{python}
#| label: impute-total-charges
df = df_raw.copy()
df["TotalCharges"] = df["TotalCharges"].fillna(0.0)
assert df["TotalCharges"].isna().sum() == 0
```

**Duplicate check:**

```{python}
#| label: duplicates
print(f"Duplicate customerID rows: {df['customerID'].duplicated().sum()}")
```

**Target encoding.** `Churn` (`Yes`/`No`) is mapped to `1`/`0` for the classification task.
`MonthlyCharges` is already numeric and is used directly as the regression target.

```{python}
#| label: target-encode
df["Churn"] = df["Churn"].map({"Yes": 1, "No": 0})
df["Churn"].value_counts(normalize=True)
```

**Outlier check on numeric columns.** `tenure`, `MonthlyCharges`, and `TotalCharges` are bounded,
physically-meaningful quantities (months of service, dollars billed) with no negative or
absurdly large values — a box plot / IQR check confirms no extreme outliers requiring treatment.

```{python}
#| label: outlier-check
numeric_cols = ["tenure", "MonthlyCharges", "TotalCharges"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 3))
for ax, col in zip(axes, numeric_cols):
    sns.boxplot(y=df[col], ax=ax)
    ax.set_title(col)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

No values fall outside physically plausible ranges (e.g. `tenure` is capped at 72 months,
matching a 6-year-old dataset snapshot), so no rows are dropped as outliers. Scaling of numeric
features is deferred to the modeling pipeline (Section 5), where a `StandardScaler` is fit
**inside** the training fold only, to avoid leakage.

## 4. Exploratory data analysis

### 4.1 Target balance (classification)

```{python}
#| label: churn-balance
churn_rate = df["Churn"].mean()
ax = sns.countplot(x=df["Churn"].map({0: "No", 1: "Yes"}))
ax.set_title(f"Churn class balance (overall churn rate = {churn_rate:.1%})")
ax.set_xlabel("Churn")
plt.show()
```

The classes are imbalanced (~26.5% churn), which we account for later with **stratified splits**
and by reporting precision/recall/F1/ROC-AUC rather than accuracy alone.

### 4.2 Target distribution (regression)

```{python}
#| label: monthly-charges-dist
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.histplot(df["MonthlyCharges"], bins=40, kde=True, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Distribution of MonthlyCharges")
plt.show()
df["MonthlyCharges"].describe()
```

The distribution is multi-modal rather than normal — a hint that `MonthlyCharges` is driven by
discrete add-on combinations (internet type, streaming add-ons, etc.) rather than one smooth
underlying process.

### 4.3 Numeric correlations

```{python}
#| label: corr-heatmap
corr_cols = ["tenure", "MonthlyCharges", "TotalCharges", "SeniorCitizen", "Churn"]
corr = df[corr_cols].corr()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 4))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="coolwarm", center=0, ax=ax)
plt.title("Correlation matrix")
plt.show()
```

`TotalCharges` and `tenure` are highly correlated (customers pay more in total the longer they
stay) — expected, since `TotalCharges` is roughly a running total of `MonthlyCharges` over
`tenure`. This is a **leakage risk for the regression task**: if we're predicting
`MonthlyCharges`, `TotalCharges` implicitly encodes it (`TotalCharges / tenure ≈ MonthlyCharges`).
We drop `TotalCharges` from the regression feature set for this reason (kept for the
classification task, where it's a legitimate predictor of churn behavior, not the target itself).

### 4.4 Feature vs. churn relationships

```{python}
#| label: tenure-vs-churn
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.kdeplot(data=df, x="tenure", hue=df["Churn"].map({0: "No", 1: "Yes"}), fill=True, common_norm=False, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Tenure distribution by churn status")
plt.show()
```

Churned customers are heavily concentrated at **low tenure** — churn is largely a
new-customer phenomenon, consistent with the well-known "early cancellation" pattern in
subscription businesses.

```{python}
#| label: contract-vs-churn
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
churn_by_contract = df.groupby("Contract")["Churn"].mean().sort_values()
sns.barplot(x=churn_by_contract.values, y=churn_by_contract.index, ax=ax)
ax.set_xlabel("Churn rate")
ax.set_title("Churn rate by contract type")
plt.show()
```

Month-to-month contracts churn far more than one/two-year contracts — an expected but
important business signal (contract length is a strong lever for retention).

```{python}
#| label: internet-vs-charges
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x="InternetService", y="MonthlyCharges", ax=ax)
ax.set_title("MonthlyCharges by InternetService type")
plt.show()
```

Fiber optic customers pay noticeably more per month than DSL or no-internet customers —
`InternetService` is likely to be an important feature for the regression task.

## 5. Cleaned dataset checkpoint

```{python}
#| label: save-checkpoint
df.drop(columns=["customerID"], inplace=True)
print(df.shape)
df.to_parquet("../data/telco_clean.parquet")
df.head(3)
```

The cleaned dataset (`data/telco_clean.parquet`) is the input to feature engineering and
modeling in the next sections.

## 6. Feature engineering

All engineered features below are **deterministic transformations of existing raw columns**
(ratios, counts, binning, boolean flags) — none of them are *fit* on the data (no learned
statistics), so they can safely be computed before the train/test split without leaking
information across the split. Anything that *is* fit (scalers, one-hot encoders, feature
selectors) is fit inside the training fold only, via a `Pipeline` in Section 8.

```{python}
#| label: feature-engineering
addon_cols = [
    "OnlineSecurity", "OnlineBackup", "DeviceProtection",
    "TechSupport", "StreamingTV", "StreamingMovies",
]

df_feat = df.copy()

# Ratio / scaling-style feature
df_feat["tenure_years"] = df_feat["tenure"] / 12.0

# Count-based domain feature: how many add-on services the customer has
df_feat["num_addon_services"] = (df_feat[addon_cols] == "Yes").sum(axis=1)

# Boolean domain flags
df_feat["has_internet"] = (df_feat["InternetService"] != "No").astype(int)
df_feat["has_family"] = ((df_feat["Partner"] == "Yes") | (df_feat["Dependents"] == "Yes")).astype(int)
df_feat["is_new_customer"] = (df_feat["tenure"] <= 3).astype(int)

# Binning: tenure into lifecycle stages (nonlinear signal seen in Section 4.4)
df_feat["tenure_bucket"] = pd.cut(
    df_feat["tenure"], bins=[-1, 12, 24, 48, 60, 72],
    labels=["0-1yr", "1-2yr", "2-4yr", "4-5yr", "5-6yr"],
)

# Historical spend rate — only valid as a feature for the CLASSIFICATION task.
# For the regression task (predicting MonthlyCharges) this would be leakage, since
# TotalCharges / tenure ~= MonthlyCharges almost by construction (Section 4.3).
df_feat["avg_monthly_spend_to_date"] = df_feat["TotalCharges"] / df_feat["tenure"].replace(0, 1)

df_feat.filter(regex="tenure|num_addon|has_|is_new|avg_monthly").head()
```

### Task-specific feature sets

The two tasks need **different** feature sets to stay leakage-safe:

- **Classification (`Churn`)** — may use `MonthlyCharges`, `TotalCharges`, and
  `avg_monthly_spend_to_date`; these describe the customer's *current* billing situation, which
  is legitimately known before we'd want to predict whether they'll churn.
- **Regression (`MonthlyCharges`)** — must drop `TotalCharges` and `avg_monthly_spend_to_date`
  (near-deterministic functions of the target, per Section 4.3), and must drop `Churn` (an
  outcome that happens *after*, not before, a customer's plan determines their bill).

```{python}
#| label: task-feature-sets
drop_for_regression = ["MonthlyCharges", "TotalCharges", "avg_monthly_spend_to_date", "Churn"]
drop_for_classification = ["Churn"]

X_clf = df_feat.drop(columns=drop_for_classification)
y_clf = df_feat["Churn"]

X_reg = df_feat.drop(columns=drop_for_regression)
y_reg = df_feat["MonthlyCharges"]

categorical_cols = X_reg.select_dtypes(include=["object", "category"]).columns.tolist()
numeric_cols_clf = X_clf.select_dtypes(include=["int64", "float64"]).columns.tolist()
numeric_cols_reg = X_reg.select_dtypes(include=["int64", "float64"]).columns.tolist()

print("Classification features:", X_clf.shape[1])
print("Regression features:", X_reg.shape[1])
print("Categorical columns:", categorical_cols)
```

## 7. Train / validation / test split

A **stratified** split is used for the classification target (to preserve the ~26.5% churn
rate in every split — important given the class imbalance from Section 4.1). Both tasks share
the same row-level split (same customers in train/val/test for both tasks), which keeps the
report simple without affecting either task's validity, since the split is stratified on the
classification target and shuffled randomly with respect to `MonthlyCharges`.

We use a 60/20/20 train/validation/test split: the validation set supports hyperparameter
tuning and overfitting checks (Section 9), and the test set is touched only for final
leaderboard evaluation (Section 10).

```{python}
#| label: train-val-test-split
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

idx_train, idx_temp = train_test_split(
    df_feat.index, test_size=0.4, stratify=y_clf, random_state=RANDOM_STATE,
)
idx_val, idx_test = train_test_split(
    idx_temp, test_size=0.5, stratify=y_clf.loc[idx_temp], random_state=RANDOM_STATE,
)

print(f"train={len(idx_train)}  val={len(idx_val)}  test={len(idx_test)}")
print("Churn rate — train/val/test:",
      y_clf.loc[idx_train].mean().round(3),
      y_clf.loc[idx_val].mean().round(3),
      y_clf.loc[idx_test].mean().round(3))
```

```{python}
#| label: apply-split
X_clf_train, y_clf_train = X_clf.loc[idx_train], y_clf.loc[idx_train]
X_clf_val, y_clf_val = X_clf.loc[idx_val], y_clf.loc[idx_val]
X_clf_test, y_clf_test = X_clf.loc[idx_test], y_clf.loc[idx_test]

X_reg_train, y_reg_train = X_reg.loc[idx_train], y_reg.loc[idx_train]
X_reg_val, y_reg_val = X_reg.loc[idx_val], y_reg.loc[idx_val]
X_reg_test, y_reg_test = X_reg.loc[idx_test], y_reg.loc[idx_test]
```

## 8. Leakage-safe preprocessing & feature selection

Preprocessing (scaling, one-hot encoding) and feature selection are wrapped in a
`ColumnTransformer` + `Pipeline`, fit only via `.fit()` on the **training** split. Validation
and test data only ever go through `.transform()`, never `.fit()` — this is what makes the
pipeline leakage-safe regardless of which model is plugged in downstream.

Feature selection method: an **L1-penalized (Lasso-style) linear model** is used as an
embedded selector (`SelectFromModel`) — `LogisticRegression(penalty="l1")` for classification,
`Lasso` for regression — run only on the training fold, to drop features (mostly one-hot
categories) that contribute little signal.

```{python}
#| label: preprocessing-pipelines
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFromModel
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Lasso

def make_preprocessor(numeric_cols, categorical_cols):
    return ColumnTransformer(
        transformers=[
            ("num", StandardScaler(), numeric_cols),
            ("cat", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"), categorical_cols),
        ]
    )

preprocessor_clf = make_preprocessor(numeric_cols_clf, categorical_cols)
preprocessor_reg = make_preprocessor(numeric_cols_reg, categorical_cols)

selector_clf = SelectFromModel(
    LogisticRegression(penalty="l1", solver="liblinear", C=0.5, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    threshold="median",
)
selector_reg = SelectFromModel(
    Lasso(alpha=0.1, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    threshold="median",
)

feature_selection_clf = Pipeline([
    ("preprocess", preprocessor_clf),
    ("select", selector_clf),
])
feature_selection_reg = Pipeline([
    ("preprocess", preprocessor_reg),
    ("select", selector_reg),
])

n_before_clf = feature_selection_clf.fit_transform(X_clf_train, y_clf_train).shape[1]
n_total_clf = feature_selection_clf.named_steps["preprocess"].fit_transform(X_clf_train).shape[1]
print(f"Classification: {n_total_clf} encoded features -> {n_before_clf} selected (median threshold)")

n_before_reg = feature_selection_reg.fit_transform(X_reg_train, y_reg_train).shape[1]
n_total_reg = feature_selection_reg.named_steps["preprocess"].fit_transform(X_reg_train).shape[1]
print(f"Regression: {n_total_reg} encoded features -> {n_before_reg} selected (median threshold)")
```

These `preprocessor_*` / `SelectFromModel` steps are reused as the first stages of every model
pipeline in Section 9, so every model — baseline through XGBoost — sees exactly the same
leakage-safe preprocessing, fit fresh on the training fold each time.

```{python}
#| label: pipeline-helper
from sklearn.base import clone

def build_pipeline(preprocessor, selector, model):
    return Pipeline([
        ("preprocess", clone(preprocessor)),
        ("select", clone(selector)),
        ("model", model),
    ])
```

## 9. Model ladder — Classification (`Churn`)

Four models, simple to advanced, all trained on the **same** leakage-safe preprocessing:

1. `DummyClassifier` (baseline — predicts the majority class every time)
2. `LogisticRegression` (linear model)
3. `DecisionTreeClassifier` (single tree — we expect this to overfit)
4. `XGBClassifier` (gradient-boosted trees, with cross-validated hyperparameter tuning)

```{python}
#| label: clf-baseline
from sklearn.dummy import DummyClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression as LogReg
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, f1_score

clf_models = {}

clf_models["Dummy"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_clf, selector_clf, DummyClassifier(strategy="most_frequent", random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
clf_models["Logistic Regression"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_clf, selector_clf, LogReg(max_iter=2000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
clf_models["Decision Tree"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_clf, selector_clf, DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))

for name, pipe in clf_models.items():
    pipe.fit(X_clf_train, y_clf_train)
    train_acc = accuracy_score(y_clf_train, pipe.predict(X_clf_train))
    val_acc = accuracy_score(y_clf_val, pipe.predict(X_clf_val))
    print(f"{name:22s}  train_acc={train_acc:.3f}  val_acc={val_acc:.3f}")
```

The unrestricted **Decision Tree** shows the classic overfitting signature: near-perfect
training accuracy but a noticeably lower validation accuracy — it has memorized the training
data rather than learning generalizable patterns.

### 9.1 Overfitting vs. underfitting — validation curve

To make the overfitting/underfitting tradeoff concrete, we sweep the Decision Tree's `max_depth`
and track train vs. validation accuracy at each depth:

```{python}
#| label: clf-validation-curve
depths = range(1, 21)
train_scores, val_scores = [], []
for d in depths:
    pipe = build_pipeline(preprocessor_clf, selector_clf, DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=d, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
    pipe.fit(X_clf_train, y_clf_train)
    train_scores.append(accuracy_score(y_clf_train, pipe.predict(X_clf_train)))
    val_scores.append(accuracy_score(y_clf_val, pipe.predict(X_clf_val)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.plot(list(depths), train_scores, marker="o", label="Train accuracy")
ax.plot(list(depths), val_scores, marker="o", label="Validation accuracy")
ax.set_xlabel("max_depth")
ax.set_ylabel("Accuracy")
ax.set_title("Decision Tree: train vs. validation accuracy by depth")
ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

At shallow depths (1-3) both curves are low and close together — **underfitting** (the model is
too simple to capture the pattern). As depth grows past ~6-8, train accuracy keeps climbing
toward 1.0 while validation accuracy plateaus and then drifts down — **overfitting** (the model
starts memorizing training noise). This is exactly why XGBoost below is tuned with
cross-validation rather than trained at full, unconstrained depth.

### 9.2 XGBoost — cross-validated tuning

```{python}
#| label: clf-xgboost
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

xgb_clf_pipe = build_pipeline(
    preprocessor_clf, selector_clf,
    XGBClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE, eval_metric="logloss"),
)

param_grid_clf = {
    "model__n_estimators": [100, 300],
    "model__max_depth": [3, 5],
    "model__learning_rate": [0.05, 0.1],
}

grid_clf = GridSearchCV(xgb_clf_pipe, param_grid_clf, cv=3, scoring="roc_auc", n_jobs=1)
grid_clf.fit(X_clf_train, y_clf_train)

print("Best params:", grid_clf.best_params_)
print(f"Best CV ROC-AUC: {grid_clf.best_score_:.3f}")

clf_models["XGBoost"] = grid_clf.best_estimator_
train_acc = accuracy_score(y_clf_train, clf_models["XGBoost"].predict(X_clf_train))
val_acc = accuracy_score(y_clf_val, clf_models["XGBoost"].predict(X_clf_val))
print(f"XGBoost  train_acc={train_acc:.3f}  val_acc={val_acc:.3f}")
```

The gap between train and validation accuracy for the tuned XGBoost model is much smaller than
the unrestricted Decision Tree's gap — cross-validated tuning is doing its job of controlling
overfitting.

## 10. Model ladder — Regression (`MonthlyCharges`)

Same ladder structure, for the regression task:

1. `DummyRegressor` (baseline — predicts the mean every time)
2. `Ridge` (linear model, L2-regularized)
3. `DecisionTreeRegressor`
4. `XGBRegressor` (with cross-validated tuning)

```{python}
#| label: reg-baseline
from sklearn.dummy import DummyRegressor
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeRegressor
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, root_mean_squared_error, r2_score

reg_models = {}
reg_models["Dummy"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_reg, selector_reg, DummyRegressor(strategy="mean"))
reg_models["Ridge"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_reg, selector_reg, Ridge(alpha=1.0, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
reg_models["Decision Tree"] = build_pipeline(preprocessor_reg, selector_reg, DecisionTreeRegressor(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))

for name, pipe in reg_models.items():
    pipe.fit(X_reg_train, y_reg_train)
    train_r2 = r2_score(y_reg_train, pipe.predict(X_reg_train))
    val_r2 = r2_score(y_reg_val, pipe.predict(X_reg_val))
    print(f"{name:15s}  train_R2={train_r2:.3f}  val_R2={val_r2:.3f}")
```

As with classification, the unrestricted Decision Tree overfits (train R² close to 1.0, a
noticeably lower validation R²).

```{python}
#| label: reg-xgboost
from xgboost import XGBRegressor

xgb_reg_pipe = build_pipeline(preprocessor_reg, selector_reg, XGBRegressor(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))

param_grid_reg = {
    "model__n_estimators": [100, 300],
    "model__max_depth": [3, 5],
    "model__learning_rate": [0.05, 0.1],
}

grid_reg = GridSearchCV(xgb_reg_pipe, param_grid_reg, cv=3, scoring="r2", n_jobs=1)
grid_reg.fit(X_reg_train, y_reg_train)

print("Best params:", grid_reg.best_params_)
print(f"Best CV R2: {grid_reg.best_score_:.3f}")

reg_models["XGBoost"] = grid_reg.best_estimator_
train_r2 = r2_score(y_reg_train, reg_models["XGBoost"].predict(X_reg_train))
val_r2 = r2_score(y_reg_val, reg_models["XGBoost"].predict(X_reg_val))
print(f"XGBoost  train_R2={train_r2:.3f}  val_R2={val_r2:.3f}")
```

## 11. Benchmark leaderboard & honest evaluation (test set)

The test set has not been touched by any model or tuning step until now. To keep it that way, the
**winning model for each task is selected using the validation set**, not the test set — the test
set is used only to report final, honest performance for whichever model validation already
picked, exactly once. Picking a winner directly from test-set numbers is a subtle second use of
supposedly-held-out data (it's not the same as leakage, but it does mean the reported test score
is no longer a fully unbiased read of real-world performance) — selecting on validation instead
avoids that.

### 11.0 Selecting the winner on the validation set

```{python}
#| label: model-selection-on-validation
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score

clf_val_scores = {}
for name, pipe in clf_models.items():
    probs_val = pipe.predict_proba(X_clf_val)[:, 1] if hasattr(pipe, "predict_proba") else None
    clf_val_scores[name] = roc_auc_score(y_clf_val, probs_val) if probs_val is not None else float("nan")

reg_val_scores = {}
for name, pipe in reg_models.items():
    reg_val_scores[name] = r2_score(y_reg_val, pipe.predict(X_reg_val))

best_clf_name = max(clf_val_scores, key=clf_val_scores.get)
best_reg_name = max(reg_val_scores, key=reg_val_scores.get)

print("Validation ROC-AUC by model:", {k: round(v, 4) for k, v in clf_val_scores.items()})
print(f"-> selected winner (classification): {best_clf_name}")
print("Validation R2 by model:       ", {k: round(v, 4) for k, v in reg_val_scores.items()})
print(f"-> selected winner (regression):    {best_reg_name}")
```

### 11.1 Classification leaderboard

```{python}
#| label: clf-leaderboard
from sklearn.metrics import precision_score, recall_score, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix

clf_leaderboard = []
for name, pipe in clf_models.items():
    preds = pipe.predict(X_clf_test)
    probs = pipe.predict_proba(X_clf_test)[:, 1] if hasattr(pipe, "predict_proba") else None
    clf_leaderboard.append({
        "model": name,
        "accuracy": accuracy_score(y_clf_test, preds),
        "precision": precision_score(y_clf_test, preds, zero_division=0),
        "recall": recall_score(y_clf_test, preds, zero_division=0),
        "f1": f1_score(y_clf_test, preds, zero_division=0),
        "roc_auc": roc_auc_score(y_clf_test, probs) if probs is not None else float("nan"),
    })

clf_leaderboard_df = pd.DataFrame(clf_leaderboard).set_index("model").round(3)
clf_leaderboard_df
```

```{python}
#| label: clf-confusion-matrix
best_clf_pipe = clf_models[best_clf_name]
cm = confusion_matrix(y_clf_test, best_clf_pipe.predict(X_clf_test))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 4))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues", xticklabels=["No churn", "Churn"], yticklabels=["No churn", "Churn"], ax=ax)
ax.set_xlabel("Predicted")
ax.set_ylabel("Actual")
ax.set_title(f"Confusion matrix — {best_clf_name} (test set)")
plt.show()
```

**Honest read:** every model beats the `Dummy` baseline (which by construction gets
`accuracy ≈ 1 - churn_rate ≈ 0.735` but `precision`/`recall`/`f1` of 0 for the churn class,
since it never predicts churn at all — a reminder that accuracy alone would have hidden how
useless the baseline actually is here). **`{python} best_clf_name`** was selected on the
validation set (Section 11.0) and confirmed here on test. Its test ROC-AUC (0.837) and XGBoost's
(0.832) are separated by 0.005 — on a ~1,400-row test set that gap is not statistically
meaningful (bootstrapped confidence intervals for the two would overlap substantially), so the
honest description is **effectively tied**, not "Logistic Regression wins." We select the simpler,
more interpretable linear model as the practical choice given that tie, which is itself a useful
finding: this is a dataset where a well-regularized linear model already captures most of the
available signal, and more model complexity buys essentially nothing.

### 11.1.1 Choosing a decision threshold

Every metric above uses the default 0.5 probability cutoff ("flag as churn if probability ≥ 50%")
— an arbitrary convention, not a business decision. At 0.5, `{python} best_clf_name` catches only
about half of actual churners (recall = 0.503, from the leaderboard above). For a retention use
case, missing a real churner (lost revenue, no chance to intervene) is usually more costly than
a false alarm (an unnecessary retention offer to a customer who was staying anyway) — so a lower
threshold that trades some precision for more recall is worth considering. This tuning is done on
the **validation** set, consistent with Section 11.0 — the test set is touched only once, to
confirm the final choice.

```{python}
#| label: threshold-analysis
from sklearn.metrics import precision_recall_curve

probs_val_best = best_clf_pipe.predict_proba(X_clf_val)[:, 1]
prec_curve, rec_curve, thresh_curve = precision_recall_curve(y_clf_val, probs_val_best)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.plot(thresh_curve, prec_curve[:-1], label="Precision")
ax.plot(thresh_curve, rec_curve[:-1], label="Recall")
ax.axvline(0.5, color="gray", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6, label="Default (0.5)")
ax.set_xlabel("Decision threshold")
ax.set_ylabel("Score")
ax.set_title(f"Precision / recall vs. threshold — {best_clf_name} (validation set)")
ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: threshold-pick
target_recall = 0.70
candidates = np.where(rec_curve[:-1] >= target_recall)[0]
chosen_threshold = float(thresh_curve[candidates[-1]]) if len(candidates) else 0.5
chosen_precision = float(prec_curve[:-1][candidates[-1]]) if len(candidates) else None
chosen_recall = float(rec_curve[:-1][candidates[-1]]) if len(candidates) else None

print(f"Chosen threshold (validation set): {chosen_threshold:.3f}")
print(f"At this threshold on validation -> precision: {chosen_precision:.3f}, recall: {chosen_recall:.3f}")

test_probs_best = best_clf_pipe.predict_proba(X_clf_test)[:, 1]
test_preds_chosen = (test_probs_best >= chosen_threshold).astype(int)
print(
    f"Confirmed on test set -> precision: {precision_score(y_clf_test, test_preds_chosen, zero_division=0):.3f}, "
    f"recall: {recall_score(y_clf_test, test_preds_chosen, zero_division=0):.3f}, "
    f"f1: {f1_score(y_clf_test, test_preds_chosen, zero_division=0):.3f}"
)
```

**Decision:** we lower the deployed threshold to **`{python} round(chosen_threshold, 2)`** (from the
default 0.5) to prioritize catching more real churners, accepting more false alarms in exchange.
This is a genuine business tradeoff, not a modeling improvement — the model itself is unchanged,
only the cutoff applied to its output. The deployed app uses this threshold for its binary
"at risk" flag, while still showing the raw probability so a human can apply their own judgment
regardless of where the cutoff sits.

### 11.2 Regression leaderboard

```{python}
#| label: reg-leaderboard
reg_leaderboard = []
for name, pipe in reg_models.items():
    preds = pipe.predict(X_reg_test)
    reg_leaderboard.append({
        "model": name,
        "R2": r2_score(y_reg_test, preds),
        "MAE": mean_absolute_error(y_reg_test, preds),
        "RMSE": root_mean_squared_error(y_reg_test, preds),
    })

reg_leaderboard_df = pd.DataFrame(reg_leaderboard).set_index("model").round(3)
reg_leaderboard_df
```

```{python}
#| label: reg-pred-vs-actual
best_reg_pipe = reg_models[best_reg_name]
preds_best = best_reg_pipe.predict(X_reg_test)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 5))
ax.scatter(y_reg_test, preds_best, alpha=0.3, s=15)
lims = [y_reg_test.min(), y_reg_test.max()]
ax.plot(lims, lims, "r--", label="Perfect prediction")
ax.set_xlabel("Actual MonthlyCharges")
ax.set_ylabel("Predicted MonthlyCharges")
ax.set_title(f"Predicted vs. actual — {best_reg_name} (test set)")
ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

**Honest read:** the `Dummy` regressor (predicts the mean every time) sets the floor with
`R2 ≈ 0`. **`{python} best_reg_name`** was selected on the validation set (Section 11.0) and
confirmed here on test — the predicted-vs-actual plot shows points tracking the diagonal closely,
with slightly more scatter at the high end (`MonthlyCharges` > ~$100), meaning the model is more
confident about typical bills than unusually large ones (likely because there are simply fewer
high-bill examples to learn from, per the multi-modal distribution seen in Section 4.2). Ridge and
XGBoost round to the same test R² (0.999) — another effective tie, not a real margin — so we again
prefer the simpler, fully interpretable linear model (see the recovered rate card in
Section 11.3) rather than crediting XGBoost with discovering something Ridge missed.

**A near-perfect R² (~0.999) deserves scrutiny, not celebration at face value** — this is
usually the first sign of leakage. We checked it directly: grouping customers by their exact
service configuration (`InternetService`, all six add-ons, `Contract`, `PhoneService`,
`MultipleLines`) and looking at the spread of `MonthlyCharges` *within* each group gives a
standard deviation of about **$1** on average. In other words, this telecom's billing is
(almost) an additive function of which services a customer has subscribed to — the same plan
essentially always costs the same amount, give or take rounding. None of the regression features
are derived from `MonthlyCharges` or `TotalCharges` (both were explicitly excluded in Section 6
for exactly this reason), so this is **not leakage** — it's a genuine, checkable property of how
this company prices its services, and it means the regression task is close to solvable by
construction rather than being a hard prediction problem. We report this transparently rather
than presenting the R² as evidence of a sophisticated model: a simple `Ridge` regression gets
nearly the same score as `XGBoost` here precisely because the underlying relationship is close
to linear/additive, not because either model discovered something subtle.

```{python}
#| label: regression-sanity-check
config_cols = [
    "InternetService", "OnlineSecurity", "OnlineBackup", "DeviceProtection",
    "TechSupport", "StreamingTV", "StreamingMovies", "PhoneService",
    "MultipleLines", "Contract",
]
within_group_std = df_feat.groupby(config_cols)["MonthlyCharges"].std()
print(f"Mean within-plan-configuration std of MonthlyCharges: ${within_group_std.mean():.2f}")
print(f"Share of configurations with std < $1: {(within_group_std < 1).mean():.1%}")
```

### 11.3 Ruling out overfitting in the regression model

A test R² this close to 1.0 is unusual enough that the rate-card explanation above deserves
independent proof, not just a plausible story. Overfitting has a precise definition — a model
that fits training-set noise so well it fails to generalize, visible as a **large gap between
training and held-out performance**. The four checks below test for that directly, rather than
re-asserting the domain explanation.

**Check 1 — train vs. validation vs. test R², side by side.** If the winning model were
overfitting, training performance would be noticeably higher than validation/test performance.

```{python}
#| label: reg-overfitting-check-1
r2_train = r2_score(y_reg_train, best_reg_pipe.predict(X_reg_train))
r2_val = r2_score(y_reg_val, best_reg_pipe.predict(X_reg_val))
r2_test = r2_score(y_reg_test, best_reg_pipe.predict(X_reg_test))

print(f"Train R2:      {r2_train:.4f}")
print(f"Validation R2: {r2_val:.4f}")
print(f"Test R2:       {r2_test:.4f}")
print(f"Train-test gap: {r2_train - r2_test:+.4f}")
```

All three numbers sit within a few thousandths of each other. **This is the opposite signature of
overfitting** — an overfit model shows a large train/held-out gap; here there is essentially none,
because an additive rate-card relationship is genuinely easy to learn without memorizing noise.

**Check 2 — 5-fold cross-validation across the whole dataset.** A single lucky train/test split
could in principle produce an inflated score. Cross-validation refits the model on 5 different
partitions and reports the spread.

```{python}
#| label: reg-overfitting-check-2
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

cv_scores = cross_val_score(clone(best_reg_pipe), X_reg, y_reg, cv=5, scoring="r2")
print("R2 per fold:", np.round(cv_scores, 4))
print(f"Mean R2: {cv_scores.mean():.4f}  (std: {cv_scores.std():.4f})")
```

The score is stable to the third decimal place across five independent folds — not a fluke of one
particular split.

**Check 3 — negative control (permutation test).** This is the most direct test for a leaking or
buggy pipeline: randomly permute `MonthlyCharges` so any real relationship between features and
target is destroyed, then refit and score on the (real, untouched) test set. If the pipeline could
still score well after this, it would mean it is exploiting something other than genuine signal —
e.g. a bug letting it see the answer. A collapse to ~0 (or negative) R² shows it cannot
manufacture a score from nothing.

```{python}
#| label: reg-overfitting-check-3
rng = np.random.RandomState(RANDOM_STATE)
y_reg_train_shuffled = rng.permutation(y_reg_train.values)

shuffled_pipe = clone(best_reg_pipe)
shuffled_pipe.fit(X_reg_train, y_reg_train_shuffled)
r2_shuffled = r2_score(y_reg_test, shuffled_pipe.predict(X_reg_test))

print(f"R2 on real test set, trained on shuffled target: {r2_shuffled:.4f}")
```

With the training target shuffled, R² collapses — confirming the 0.999 score on the real target
reflects a genuine, learnable relationship rather than an artifact of the pipeline, the split, or
a hidden leakage path.

**Check 4 — the recovered rate card.** `Ridge` is linear, so its coefficients *are* the model —
there is no hidden complexity to distrust. Below is the model's learned price effect for each
service/plan feature, recovered by name and checkable by hand.

```{python}
#| label: reg-rate-card
reg_model_step = best_reg_pipe.named_steps["model"]
reg_feature_names = best_reg_pipe.named_steps["preprocess"].get_feature_names_out()
reg_selected_mask = best_reg_pipe.named_steps["select"].get_support()
reg_selected_names = reg_feature_names[reg_selected_mask]
clean_reg_names = [
    name.replace("num__", "").replace("cat__", "").replace("_", " ")
    for name in reg_selected_names
]

if hasattr(reg_model_step, "coef_"):
    rate_card = pd.DataFrame({
        "feature": clean_reg_names,
        "price_effect_$": reg_model_step.coef_,
    }).sort_values("price_effect_$", ascending=False)
    print(f"Base rate (intercept): ${reg_model_step.intercept_:.2f}\n")
    print(rate_card.round(2).to_string(index=False))
else:
    importances = reg_model_step.feature_importances_
    rate_card = pd.DataFrame({
        "feature": clean_reg_names,
        "importance": importances,
    }).sort_values("importance", ascending=False)
    print(rate_card.round(4).to_string(index=False))
```

This is the plainest possible answer to "is this overfitting?" — a short table of interpretable
dollar coefficients that visibly matches the company's actual pricing structure (fiber optic and
streaming add-ons carry a positive price effect; DSL-only or no-internet plans carry a negative
one relative to the baseline). A model that memorized noise would not produce coefficients this
clean or this consistent with what the features actually mean. Combined with Checks 1–3, the
high R² is best read as evidence that this company's billing is close to a deterministic,
low-dimensional function of plan choice — not evidence of a flawed evaluation.

### 11.4 What matters, and what we would not trust this model to do

```{python}
#| label: feature-importance
xgb_step = clf_models["XGBoost"].named_steps["model"]
importances = xgb_step.feature_importances_

# Recover real column names: ColumnTransformer names every encoded column
# (e.g. "cat__Contract_Month-to-month"), and SelectFromModel's boolean mask
# tells us which of those survived selection — together they let us label
# the plot with real feature names instead of bare indices.
all_feature_names = clf_models["XGBoost"].named_steps["preprocess"].get_feature_names_out()
selected_mask = clf_models["XGBoost"].named_steps["select"].get_support()
selected_feature_names = all_feature_names[selected_mask]
clean_names = [
    name.replace("num__", "").replace("cat__", "").replace("_", " ")
    for name in selected_feature_names
]

top_n = min(10, len(importances))
top_idx = np.argsort(importances)[-top_n:][::-1]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.barh(range(top_n), importances[top_idx][::-1])
ax.set_yticks(range(top_n))
ax.set_yticklabels([clean_names[i] for i in top_idx][::-1])
ax.set_xlabel("XGBoost feature importance")
ax.set_title("Top churn-predicting features")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Business interpretation:**

- Both the EDA (Section 4.4) and the churn model agree: **contract length** and **tenure** are
  the strongest churn signals. A retention strategy targeting month-to-month customers in their
  first few months of service would likely have the highest ROI.
- The `MonthlyCharges` regression model is useful as a **pricing sanity check** — flagging
  customers whose actual bill deviates a lot from what their service mix would predict — but
  **we would not trust it** to set prices unsupervised, since it wasn't trained to account for
  promotional discounts, loyalty pricing, or negotiated rates, none of which are present as
  columns in this dataset.
- Neither model should be used to make automated retention *decisions* (e.g. auto-cancelling a
  contract or auto-adjusting a bill) — they're decision-support tools for a human, not
  replacements for one, given the moderate (not exceptional) performance margins over the
  baselines shown above.

## 12. Saving the final pipelines for deployment

The best pipeline per task, as selected on the test-set leaderboard above, is refit on the
**full dataset** (train + validation + test) before saving — since test performance has already
been honestly reported, there is no reason to withhold that data from the model that will
actually serve predictions in the app.

```{python}
#| label: save-models
import joblib
import os

os.makedirs("../models", exist_ok=True)

final_clf_pipe = clone(best_clf_pipe)
final_clf_pipe.fit(X_clf, y_clf)
joblib.dump(final_clf_pipe, "../models/churn_pipeline.pkl")

final_reg_pipe = clone(best_reg_pipe)
final_reg_pipe.fit(X_reg, y_reg)
joblib.dump(final_reg_pipe, "../models/charges_pipeline.pkl")

print(f"Saved churn_pipeline.pkl (best model: {best_clf_name})")
print(f"Saved charges_pipeline.pkl (best model: {best_reg_name})")
print("Classification feature columns:", list(X_clf.columns))
print("Regression feature columns:", list(X_reg.columns))
```
